steps.n: This is one of the mysterious letters that appear at the beginning of some chapters in the Quran. Its exact meaning is considered unknown.
Added the elongation mark (مد) which indicates this letter is pronounced longer when recited. This is a common feature in the opening letters of surahs.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the noun for the tool used in writing
Adding 'ال' makes it refer to a specific pen rather than any pen
Adding و as an oath particle (meaning 'by') rather than just 'and'. In Quranic Arabic, و at the start of an oath means 'by' or 'I swear by'
The pen gets a kasra (i) sound at the end because it's part of an oath starting with wa (and)
This is a word that refers to something general - in this case, it refers to what is being written
The 'وَ' connects this to the previous oath with نٓ وَٱلْقَلَمِ (Nun and the pen)
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and show past tense meaning
The ي prefix indicates 3rd person masculine singular in present tense

steps.m: they write in lines (referring to angels recording deeds)
steps.n: Changed from 'he writes' to 'they write' by adding ون at the end - refers to multiple angels recording deeds
This is a negative particle that makes statements negative, like 'not' in English
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation
Extended the alif to show emphasis. In this ayah, it negates the word بِمَجْنُونٍ (insane), making the meaning 'you are not insane'
This is the basic form of the independent pronoun meaning 'you'
Basic 'you' gets dots above to show it's talking about one male person. Works with بِمَجْنُونٍ to say 'you are not insane'
Forms the basic noun pattern from the root letters
Changes the vowel pattern to indicate one specific blessing
Added بِ at start to mean 'by/with' and ِ at end to show it's connected to رَبِّكَ (your Lord) that comes after it
Changed from root 'nurture' to 'my Lord' - the kasra and shaddah make it mean 'my Lord' (like saying 'O my Lord!')

The كَ at the end means 'your' when speaking to one male person. This word is in the genitive case because it comes after نِعْمَة in a possession relationship, where نِعْمَة (blessing) possesses رَبِّ (Lord).

steps.m: to hide from sight, to cover up

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Added مَ at start and changed vowels to show someone affected by being hidden/covered mentally
Added tanwin (double kasra) to show this is indefinite (like saying 'an' insane one)

steps.m: by/with an insane one (describing أَنتَ - you)
steps.n: Added بِ (meaning 'with/by') at the start to say 'you are not WITH madness/insanity'

steps.m: indeed/certainly (emphasizes لَكَ لَأَجْرًا 'you truly have a reward')
steps.n: This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it more certain. In this ayah, it emphasizes that the reward is definite.

steps.m: and indeed (connects to previous statement while emphasizing لَكَ لَأَجْرًا)
steps.n: The و connects this statement about the reward to what came before, while إِنَّ continues to provide emphasis

steps.m: you (as the object of an action)
steps.n: This is a pronoun used when talking directly to one person
Added لَ at the beginning to show that you are receiving or being given something. In this ayah, it shows you are receiving the reward (أَجْرًا).

steps.m: to give a reward or payment

steps.n: Changed the root letters into a noun meaning the reward itself. This word is affected by إِنَّ which comes earlier in the sentence.
Added ًا at the end to show it's any reward (not a specific one) and that something is happening to it
Added لَ at the beginning to emphasize that this is definitely true, like saying 'surely' or 'certainly'
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make the basic word. The َ (fatha) goes on غ, and the ْ (sukun) goes on ي to show how to pronounce it.

steps.m: other than, different from (describes the reward أَجْرًا)
steps.n: Added َ (fatha) at the end to show this word is describing the reward (أَجْرًا) mentioned earlier in the verse. When a word describes another word, it matches its ending marks.

steps.m: to give, bestow, or favor with something

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Changed from 'to give' into 'something that was given' - like changing 'give' to 'given'
Added tanween kasra to show this describes the reward (أَجْرًا) mentioned earlier. The meaning becomes 'uninterrupted' because something that is truly given/bestowed isn't taken back.
This is a special word that adds emphasis and makes what follows more certain. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' in English
The و adds the meaning of 'and'
The كَ means 'you' (when speaking to one male). This word connects to لَعَلَىٰ (are truly upon) in the next part of the ayah.
This is a preposition showing something is on top of or upon something else. In this ayah, it connects to خُلُقٍ (character/nature)
The لَ adds emphasis, showing strong certainty about what follows. Here it emphasizes that the Prophet (peace be upon him) truly has this great character.
The small alif (ىٰ) is just a way of writing the long vowel sound at the end of the word. It doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic meaning of the root
The ُ marks change this from a verb into a noun meaning someone's natural character
Added tanwin kasra to show this is describing Muhammad's character in an indefinite way, meaning it's 'a character' rather than 'the character'
This is the basic meaning of the root
Forms an adjective describing the noble character/nature mentioned in the ayah
The tanween (ٍ) shows this is indefinite, like saying 'a magnificent character' rather than 'the magnificent character'

steps.m: to see or understand clearly with eyes or mind

steps.n: Added short vowels (fatha) to make the root letters into a past tense verb meaning 'he saw'
Changed to present tense and second person (you) with تُ prefix
Added سَ to show this happens in the future

steps.m: and then you will see (connected to وَيُبْصِرُونَ - and they will see)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happens as a result or sequence. This word is connected to وَيُبْصِرُونَ (and they will see) that follows it in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Present tense marker indicating ongoing/habitual action

steps.m: they see/understand clearly (referring to the disbelievers mentioned earlier)
steps.n: The ون suffix changes the subject from 'he' to 'they' (masculine plural). This refers to the disbelievers being discussed in this passage.

steps.m: and they will see/understand clearly (connected to فَسَتُبْصِرُ - 'and you will see')
steps.n: The و connects this to the previous phrase فَسَتُبْصِرُ (and you will see), showing these actions happen together in the future
Adding the vowel mark makes it readable
Added بِ at beginning to mean 'by/with'
Added ي to intensify the meaning

steps.m: by which of you (plural) (connected to ٱلْمَفْتُونُ)
steps.n: Added كُمُ to show this means 'of you all'. This word connects to ٱلْمَفْتُونُ (the afflicted) to ask 'which of you is afflicted'

steps.m: to test or try someone through hardship

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Added مَ at start and changed vowels to make it mean 'the one receiving the test' instead of 'the one doing the test'

steps.m: the one who is being tested/tried
steps.n: Added ال to point to a specific person being tested

steps.m: the one who is being tested/tried (subject of بِأَييِّكُمُ)
steps.n: Added ُ at the end to show this word is the subject of the question word بِأَييِّكُمُ (which of you)
This is an emphatic particle that begins sentences to add certainty
Adding shadda changes this from a conditional word to one that adds emphasis. In this ayah, it emphasizes that 'your Lord' (رَبَّكَ) definitely knows something.
Added fatha marks to connect the letters together

steps.m: one who completely cares for others
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who does this action perfectly and completely
The كَ at the end means 'your'. This word is affected by إِنَّ (indeed/verily) which comes before it.
This is a personal pronoun that refers back to رَبَّكَ (your Lord) and acts as the subject of أَعْلَمُ (knows best)
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to the superlative form to show Allah is the most knowing of all. This word is describing هُوَ (He/Allah) in this ayah.
Combination of preposition بِ (about/with) and relative pronoun مَن (who/whoever)
Connected the letters and added fatha vowels to show this happened in the past

steps.m: he lost the right way (subject of أَعْلَمُ)
steps.n: Two identical letters (ل ل) merged into one letter with a shadda (ّ). This word means 'went astray' and is what Allah knows about in the ayah
This is a preposition showing movement or turning away from something. In this ayah, it connects to سَبِيلِهِ (His path) to show who strayed away from His path.
Added basic vowel marks to connect the root letters
Added ي to lengthen the middle vowel sound
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being possessed by something
Added هِۦ at the end to show this is His path, referring to Allah in this context
Combines the conjunction 'and' with the pronoun 'he'
Adding vowel marks to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to know'
Changed to comparative/superlative form. This word describes هُوَ (He) in the ayah, saying that He knows best.

steps.m: to guide or show the right path

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past-tense verb
Added اِ at start and ت after first letter to show someone receiving or seeking guidance
Changed from a past action (he was guided) to describing someone who is guided/follows guidance
Added ين to make it plural (more than one)
Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: with/by the ones who follow guidance
steps.n: Added بِ to show 'with' or 'by'
This is the basic negation word that tells us not to do something. In this ayah, it's connected to تُطِعِ (obey) to say 'do not obey'.
Added فَ at the beginning to connect this idea with what came before it, like saying 'so' or 'then' in English. The full meaning with the next word is 'so do not obey'.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Added تُ at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense
The weak letter ي is dropped because this is a negative command (after فَلَا). Added kasra at end to complete the command form.
The doubled ذ shows this is done strongly or repeatedly
Adding مُ at the start and ِ in the middle changes it from an action to describing someone who does that action
Adding ين at the end makes it refer to multiple people

steps.m: the deniers/rejecters (object of تُطِعِ - do not obey)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific deniers. This word is receiving the action of تُطِعِ (do not obey) in this ayah
Adding vowel marks makes this a past tense verb meaning 'he wanted'
Changed to plural by adding وا and changing vowel to damma. This shows the disbelievers (they) as a group wishing something.

steps.m: if (hypothetical condition that didn't happen, connected to وَدُّوا۟)
steps.n: This is a helper word that shows something that people wished would happen but didn't. Here it connects to وَدُّوا۟ (they wish) to show what they wished for.

steps.m: if (hypothetical condition that didn't happen, connected to وَدُّوا۟)
steps.n: Added fatha (َ) on ل and sukun (ْ) on و to show how to pronounce it correctly
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed from past tense (he softened) to present tense (soften) by changing middle vowel to 'i' sound
Added تُ at the beginning to show 'you' are doing the action. This word comes after وَدُّوا۟ (they wished) in the ayah.

steps.m: to soften, to compromise, to be lenient

steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing internal vowels
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
Added فَ to show this happens as a result of something else - connects to the previous part of the ayah
This is a negative particle that tells us not to do something. Here it's telling us not to obey.
The و at the start connects this command with previous instructions. Together with لا it tells us 'and do not obey'.
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added تُ at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense

steps.m: you obey (command: do not!) (connected to وَلَا)
steps.n: When وَلَا ('and do not') comes before this word, it drops the middle ي and gets a sukun at the end to make it mean 'do not obey!'
The damma and doubled ل work together to create a word meaning 'absolutely everything' with no exceptions

steps.m: every (object of لَا تُطِعْ - do not obey)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon by the verb لَا تُطِعْ (do not obey) in this ayah

steps.m: one who swears excessively (connected to مَّهِينٍ - despicable)
steps.n: Added ّ (shadda) on ل and ا after it to show someone who does this action a lot. This describes someone mentioned with مَّهِينٍ (despicable) in the ayah.
The ٍ at the end shows this is describing 'a' person (not 'the' person) and connects it to other descriptions of them
Added vowels and ي to make an adjective describing someone with this quality
Added two dots under at the end to show it's being described by another word
Doubling the first letter makes the meaning stronger/more intense
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added shadda (doubled middle letter) and alif to show this is someone who does this action a lot
Added tanwin kasra to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a' instead of 'the') and is being described by the words after it

steps.m: to move by foot, to walk

steps.n: Added shadda to show intensive walking

steps.m: one who walks around a lot (as a habit)
steps.n: Added alif and hamza to show this describes someone who does this action habitually

steps.m: one who walks around a lot (genitive case)
steps.n: Added the genitive case ending (kasratan) to show this word is the object of a preposition
Added a special ending sound (-in) to show this word describes someone who spreads gossip (connects to هَمَّازٍ)

steps.m: to spread harmful talk about others

steps.n: Added ي between م and م to make it mean 'the act of gossiping' instead of 'to gossip'
Added بِ at the beginning to show this is done using gossip as a tool

steps.m: with harmful gossip (describing مَّشَّآءٍ - one who walks about)
steps.n: Added ٍ at the end to show this describes the person mentioned before (مَّشَّآءٍ - one who walks about)

steps.m: to stop or block something from happening

steps.n: Connected the letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger

steps.m: one who constantly blocks/prevents (describing someone in a negative way)
steps.n: Adding alif after the doubled letter makes it describe someone who does this action all the time
Adding shaddah on first letter adds extra emphasis to the meaning
The tanween kasra shows this describes something in general terms. From the next word in the ayah, we know it's specifically talking about preventing good things.
Added vowel marks to the root letters to make the word mean 'goodness'. This goodness belongs to the 'preventer' (مَّنَّاعٍ) in this ayah.
Added ال to point to specific goodness
Added لِ meaning 'for/to'
The ل gets doubled (shadda) when connected to the word 'the good', like combining two ل together
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added مُ at start and ت after first letter to make it mean 'someone who' transgresses/goes beyond limits. Like changing 'he ran' to 'runner'
Changed ending to tanween kasra to show this describes the word مَّنَّاعٍ (preventer) in the ayah
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this is a past tense verb

steps.m: intensely sinful (describes someone who sins a lot)
steps.n: Changes from 'he sinned' to 'very sinful person' by adding ي to make it describe someone who does this action a lot

steps.m: an intensely sinful one (describing مَّنَّاعٍ - a preventer)
steps.n: The tanween (ٍ) shows this word is describing مَّنَّاعٍ (a preventer) in the ayah
The damma vowels make it a noun describing someone with this quality
Adding the double letter makes the meaning stronger

steps.m: a very harsh person (describing someone in a negative way)
steps.n: The tanween (two kasras) at the end shows this is describing someone in general, not a specific person
Connected the root letters together to form a word meaning 'after' in time or sequence
Added a fatha mark at the end to show this word is being used as an object
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this' for pointing to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (dagger alif) on top of the ذ
Adding لِ changes the meaning from 'this' (near) to 'that' (far)
Adding كَ completes the word and emphasizes that the thing being pointed to is far away

steps.m: to mark or brand with a distinguishing feature

steps.n: Forms an adjective describing someone with this negative quality permanently attached to them, like a brand or mark that can't be removed

steps.m: a marked/branded one (described alongside عُتُلٍّ/harsh person)
steps.n: The tanwin kasra (ٍ) makes this indefinite and shows it follows عُتُلٍّ (harsh person) in describing someone's bad qualities

steps.m: that (introduces the reason: because he had wealth and children)
steps.n: This particle introduces the reason why something happened. In this ayah, it explains why someone was arrogant - because (that) he had wealth and children.
This is the basic root meaning existence or being
Added basic past tense vowels to show 'he' did this action in the past
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas. This is a common change with weak middle letters but doesn't change the meaning.
This is a special word that can mean either 'this/that' or 'possessor of' depending on context. Here it means 'possessor of' because it's followed by مَالٍ (wealth).
Adding ا turns 'this' into 'owner of' - like saying 'the one who has' before listing what they own
The middle letter و changes to ا because it comes between two fathas
The tanween kasra (ٍ) at the end shows this wealth belongs to or is possessed by someone mentioned in the ayah
Added vowel marks to create the singular noun meaning 'son'
Regular masculine plural form indicating multiple sons

steps.m: and sons (connected to مَالٍ/wealth in the list)
steps.n: The و connects this word to مَالٍ (wealth) in the previous part of the ayah, making a list of 'wealth and sons'
This is a basic word used to talk about time. It's like saying 'at that time' or 'when'

steps.m: whenever (something happens in the future) (introduces تُتْلَىٰ - is recited)
steps.n: Adding ا at the end makes it talk about future time and makes it connect with what happens next. In this ayah, it introduces what happens when our verses are recited.
Connected the root letters and added vowels. The final و changes to ا because it's a weak letter at the end.
Changed to passive voice by changing vowels. Shows the action is being done to something rather than by something.

steps.m: it is recited (passive present tense of آيَاتُنَا)
steps.n: Added the superscript alif (ٰ) as a pronunciation guide. This word is describing what happens to آيَاتُنَا (our verses) in this ayah.
This is a common preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'. In this ayah, it connects to our signs/verses (ءَايٰتُنَا) that are being recited.

steps.m: upon him (referring to the person being recited to)
steps.n: Added هِ at the end to mean 'him', with يْ added between to make the words connect smoothly. This shows who the verses are being recited to.
The hamza and weak letters combine to form a long vowel sound
Adds ة at the end to make it mean 'a sign' instead of the action of showing
Regular feminine plural ending replaces taa marboota. This word is the subject of the passive verb تُتْلَى (are recited).

steps.m: our signs/verses (subject of تُتْلَى - are recited)
steps.n: Attached pronoun نَا means 'our'. This word is the subject of the passive verb تُتْلَى (are recited), so it takes the nominative case.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense form. This shows a male did the action in the past.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle

steps.m: to write in lines, to record

steps.n: Adding the three fatha marks makes this a past tense verb
Changed from 'he wrote' (verb) to 'a line' (noun) by making middle letter silent
Added أُ at start to make it mean 'written stories' instead of just 'written line'
Changed to broken plural form for multiple tales/legends
Added damma ending to show this is the subject of قَالَ (he said). The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this word.

steps.m: to come first or be at the beginning

steps.n: Adding the shadda (double letter) and vowels turns it from an action into a description of who/what is first
Adding ين makes it mean many people. The ِ shows it belongs to the word أَسٰطِيرُ (tales/stories) - like saying 'tales of the ancient ones'
Adding ال makes it point to specific ancient ones that were mentioned before, not just any ancient ones. The َ at the end shows it's part of أَسٰطِيرُ (tales of the ancients)
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The و at the beginning drops off because it's a weak letter
Added نَ at beginning to show 'we' are doing it, changed middle vowel to ِ and end to ُ to show it's happening now
Added سَ at beginning to show this will happen in the future
Added هُ at the end to show who we're marking (him)
This is the basic form of the word that means being high or elevated
Adding ى at the end turns this into a preposition that shows one thing is on top of another. Here it shows something will be put upon the nose.

steps.m: to extend or protrude (like a nose)

steps.n: Forms a noun meaning a body part or prominent feature

steps.m: the nose (subject of سَنَسِمُهُۥ - we will mark)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to show we're talking about a specific nose. This nose is what will be marked according to سَنَسِمُهُۥ (we will mark it)
Added ِ at the end because it comes after the word عَلَى (on/upon) which requires this ending
This is a special word used at the start of sentences to make what follows stronger or more certain. Here it emphasizes the testing mentioned in بَلَوْنٰهُمْ (We tested them).
The ا at the end means 'we/us'. This connects to the next word بَلَوْنٰهُمْ (we tested them) where 'we' is doing the testing.

steps.m: to test through trials or difficulties

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense
Added a sukoon mark to stop the vowel sound at the end
Added نٰ at the end to show 'we' are doing the action. This word comes after إِنَّا (indeed we) at the start of the verse.
Added هُمْ at the end to show who was tested - 'them'
This is a comparison word that shows one thing is similar to another. Here it connects two similar tests/trials.

steps.m: just as (connects two tests: بَلَوْنٰهُمْ and بَلَوْنَآ)
steps.n: Adding ما makes the comparison more specific - it's comparing exactly how one thing happened to another. Here it's comparing how Allah tested one group just as He tested another group.

steps.m: to test/try (especially to test someone's character or faith)

steps.n: Added نَآ at the end to show that 'We' (Allah) did the action. In the Quran, when Allah speaks using 'We', it shows His majesty and power.

steps.m: to accompany, be close to, or associate with

steps.n: Adding alif after first root letter to create active participle. This word is in accusative case because it is the object of بلونا (we tested). It is part of a possession construction with الجنة (the garden).
Added hamza at beginning and elongated alif to make plural. This word is the object of بَلَوْنَا (we tested) and is connected to الجَنَّةِ (the garden) to mean 'companions of the garden'.
Added fatha at end to show this word is the object being tested in the sentence.
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to show intensity of covering
Adding ة changes it from 'concealing' to 'a garden' (because gardens are covered/concealed places)
Added 'the' (ال) to make it mean THE garden (Paradise) instead of any garden
This is a time word that connects two events in the past. Here it connects to أَقْسَمُوا۟ (they swore), showing when they made their oath.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he made an oath, he swore
steps.n: Adding أ at start makes it mean 'to make/do' the action
The وا suffix shows that a group of people did the action. The small و (۟) above is just to help with pronunciation.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added لَ to show emphasis and future tense

steps.m: he will most certainly cut off
steps.n: Added نَّ for extra emphasis

steps.m: he will most certainly cut it off
steps.n: Added هَا to show what is being cut off (referring to the garden mentioned in context)
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start to make it mean 'one who' and changed inside vowels to show it's the doer of the action

steps.m: while they were entering morning time (describes their state when they swore أَقْسَمُوا)
steps.n: Added ينَ to show this describes multiple people and their state when doing another action (swearing an oath)
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يَسْتَثْنُونَ (they make exception) into 'they do not make exception'
The و at the start connects this with what came before, showing these actions happen together

steps.m: to turn aside or make an exception
steps.n: The basic meaning is about turning something away from its normal path
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added است at the beginning to change 'turning aside' into 'making an exception' - like setting something apart from the rest
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at the start and changing the ending
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
Added basic vowels to connect the letters and make it past tense
Added alif to show continuous motion of going around

steps.m: and then he circled around (followed by عَلَيْهَا - upon it)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened after something else. This word connects to عَلَيْهَا which tells us what he circled around.
This is a basic word that shows something is physically or metaphorically above something else, like saying 'the book is on the table'
The word changes shape to get ready for attaching endings, like how in English we might change 'y' to 'i' before adding endings

steps.m: upon it (referring to the garden in the story that was circled around)
steps.n: Added ها which means 'it' when talking about something feminine like a garden. This shows what was circled around in the phrase طَافَ عَلَيْهَا (circled around it)
Added vowels to the root letters to make a noun meaning 'one complete circle'
Changed the و to ا and added ئِ to show who does the action
Made the ا longer to add emphasis

steps.m: a circler/one who goes around (subject of فَطَافَ)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is indefinite and is the subject (doer) of the verb فَطَافَ (then it went around) in this ayah
Basic preposition showing something comes from somewhere else
Added emphasis with shadda because it connects to the word رَّبِّكَ (your Lord) that comes after it

steps.m: to care for, nurture, develop to completion

This is a preposition that shows direction or purpose, connecting to how the next words talk about 'how you judge'

steps.m: for you all (connects to كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ - how you judge)
steps.n: Added كُمْ which shows this is talking to multiple people, connecting to how the next words ask about 'how you all judge'
This is a question word that asks about the manner, way, or state of something. It doesn't come from a root.

These are the basic letters that carry the core meaning of writing or recording

The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) and tanween damma (ٌ) is added to show this is the subject of the verb تَدْرُسُونَ (you study) in this ayah

Added هِ at the end to show what the preposition applies to. Here it refers back to the كِتٰبٌ (book) mentioned earlier in the ayah.

Adding shadda changes this from a conditional word to an emphasis word that makes statements stronger. Here it emphasizes that something definitely belongs to them.

Added كُمْ to show this is addressing multiple people. This word is the object that receives the action of choosing (تَخَيَّرُونَ) in this ayah.
This is a basic preposition meaning 'in' or 'within'. Unlike most Arabic words, prepositions don't come from roots - they are basic building blocks of the language.
Added هِ which means 'it' and refers back to something mentioned before. This word connects to تَخَيَّرُونَ (you choose) to show what is being chosen from.
This لَ adds emphasis to show something is definitely true. It's connected to the verb تَخَيَّرُونَ (you choose) that comes after it.

steps.n: Adding مَا makes it refer to whatever things or choices are being selected. Together with لَ it emphasizes that you surely have whatever you choose.

steps.m: you all choose carefully for yourselves
steps.n: Adding ون makes it plural - now multiple people are doing the choosing

steps.m: or do (you have) (connects to previous verse asking about having a book)
steps.n: In this ayah, أَمْ connects to a previous question and introduces a new possibility. It's asking 'Or do you have oaths binding upon us?' after previously asking if they had a divine book.
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning
Added كُمْ to show this is directed at multiple people being addressed

steps.m: to make a promise or swear

steps.n: Root letters got vowels to make the word for 'oath' - like when you make a promise
Changed to broken plural form to show multiple oaths
Added tanwin dammah to show this is the subject doing something in the sentence. The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this Quranic spelling.
Basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'
The ى changes to ي with sukun when connecting to pronouns
Added نَا at the end to show this means 'us'

steps.m: to reach or arrive at something completely

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: reaching/effective (describes something that reaches its goal)

Basic preposition showing direction or movement towards something. In this ayah, it connects to يَوْمِ (day) to show 'to/until the Day'.

steps.m: the resurrection (connected to يَوْمِ - day of)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific (the resurrection), and ِ to show it connects to the word يَوْمِ (day of) before it
Basic conditional particle that shows uncertainty or conditions
Adding shadda changes this from a conditional word to one that adds emphasis. It makes what comes after it stronger and more certain. Here it emphasizes لَكُمْ (for you).
Starting with the basic preposition that shows purpose or ownership

steps.m: for you all (plural, addressing the people being spoken to)
steps.n: Added the plural 'you' pronoun كُمْ to show who is being addressed. This word connects to إِنَّ in the ayah to mean 'indeed for you all'

Adding ما changes the meaning to negation. This compound particle negates the verb تَحْكُمُونَ (you judge) that comes after it

steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show who should be asked. This connects to the next word أَيُّهُم (which of them) to form the complete question 'ask them which of them...'
Adding the vowel mark makes it readable
Added damma to show this word is the subject of the question

steps.n: Added هُم to show we're asking about which one from among them. This connects back to the هُمْ (them) in سَلْهُمْ (ask them)

Adding لِ changes 'this' to 'that' by showing something farther away
The كَ at the end points to something specific you can see or know about
The prefix بِ adds the meaning 'with' or 'by'. In this ayah, it connects to زَعِيمٌ (responsible/guarantor), showing who is responsible with/for that.

steps.m: to claim responsibility or speak on behalf of others
one who claims responsibility/speaks for others

This word connects two equal options in a question. In this ayah, it connects to a previous question, asking if something is true OR do they have partners (شُرَكَآءُ).
Basic preposition showing direction or purpose (connects to شُرَكَآءُ - partners)

Extended alif (آ) is just an orthographic variant of regular alif

Changes the verb into a noun describing someone who does the action
Makes the word plural to refer to multiple people

steps.m: one who nurtures completely (possessed by نِعْمَةٌ)
steps.n: The kasra (small diagonal line) at the end shows this word 'belongs to' or 'is connected to' another word (نِعْمَةٌ)
Adding هِۦ at the end shows this belongs to him
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The vowel changes make it passive - something done to someone

steps.m: surely would have been cast away (in response to لَّوْلَآ/if not for)
steps.n: The لَ adds emphasis and shows this is the result part of a conditional statement started by لَّوْلَآ (if not for)
Root letters with extra alif and hamza at end to make a noun meaning 'open/bare space'
Added ال to make it specific
Added a small i-sound (kasra) at the end because it comes after the word 'in/at' (في)
Added بِ at the beginning to show location
This is a personal pronoun meaning 'he'. It's the subject being described as مَذْمُومٌ (blamed/condemned) in this ayah.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it

steps.m: to blame or find fault with

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from verb (he blamed) to noun (one who is blamed)
Added tanween to show this is any blamed thing, not a specific one
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added ا at start and ت after first letter to show the action is done for oneself

steps.m: and he chose specially for himself
steps.n: Added فَ to show this action follows something before it

steps.m: and he chose specially for himself
steps.n: Added ٱ to properly start the verb after فَ

steps.m: and He chose him (his Lord chose him)
steps.n: Added هُ to show who was chosen. The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) when followed by هُ.

steps.m: to care for and help grow/develop
one who cares for and develops others completely
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) shows this is someone who does complete nurturing and development of others
The ُ (damma) shows this word is doing the action in the sentence - it's the one who chose/selected

steps.m: his Lord (the one who chose him)
steps.n: The هُ shows this is his Lord. This Lord is the one who chose him (اجْتَبٰهُ) in this verse.

steps.m: to make something into something else

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fatha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he made/turned'

steps.m: and then he made (connected to اجْتَبٰهُ)
steps.n: Added فَ at the start to show this action follows after Allah chose him (اجْتَبٰهُ)

steps.m: and then He made him (into one of الصَّالِحِينَ)
steps.n: Added هُ (him) to show who was made/turned into something. This connects to the next phrase مِنَ ٱلصَّالِحِينَ (among the righteous) which shows what he was made into.
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or what group it belongs to. Here it connects 'made him' to 'the righteous', showing he was made one of the righteous.
Added َ (fatha) at the end because the next word starts with ال. This helps connect the words smoothly when reading.
Added basic vowels to connect the letters and show past tense meaning
This form describes someone who has this quality (like saying 'a righteous one')
The ين ending makes it plural. Gets kasra (ِ) because it comes after مِنَ (from/among)
ال makes it specific - 'the' righteous people

steps.m: And if - connects ideas and introduces a condition
steps.n: This is a combination of two particles: و (and) + إن (if)

steps.m: to be close to/almost do something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense form

Added يَ at beginning to show present tense and ُ at end to show he is doing the action. The و from the root becomes ا in this form. This verb connects to لَيُزْلِقُونَكَ (they will surely make you slip) by showing they are almost doing this action.

steps.m: those who - points to specific people or things
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun used for plural masculine
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense singular form

steps.m: they disbelieved (subject of لَيُزْلِقُونَكَ - they would make you slip)
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people did this action in the past. This word is the subject of لَيُزْلِقُونَكَ (they would make you slip) in this ayah.
Adds ي at start to make it 'he' in present tense
Adds vowel marks to show it's active voice present tense
Adds a 'u' sound at the end to show it's a complete thought
The لَ adds emphasis (surely/indeed)
The ونَ indicates plural masculine subject (they)

steps.m: they would surely make you slip (acted on)
steps.n: The كَ shows you as the object being made to slip. This is connected to يَكَادُ (they almost) earlier in the verse, showing they almost make you slip with their eyes when they hear the Quran.
This word is part of how the disbelievers are trying to make the Prophet slip (يُزْلِقُونَكَ) - they're doing it with their eyes through evil glances.
Preposition بِ meaning 'with/by' attached as prefix
The word takes genitive case after the preposition بِ. The preposition بِ with this word shows how the action of 'making slip' is being done - with their eyes.
Adding possessive pronoun suffix هِمْ meaning 'their'
Starting with the basic particle form with vowel marks
Added shadda on م and added ا at the end to show this connects two events that happened in sequence. In this ayah, it connects 'they heard the Reminder' with what follows.
Connected the root letters together with vowel marks to make the past tense form for one person
Added وا at the end to show that a group of people did the action. This word connects to يَقُولُونَ (they say) in the ayah, showing the same group is doing both actions.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to make a noun representing the result of remembering or mentioning
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ذ because it comes after ال

steps.m: the reminder/message (object of سَمِعُوا۟ - they heard)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the verb سَمِعُوا۟ (they heard) in this ayah
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense form
Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Adds conjunction و to connect with previous statement: 'they almost slip you with their looks when they hear the reminder'

steps.m: and they speak/say (referring to those who disbelieved)
steps.n: Adds plural ending to show multiple people (the disbelievers) are speaking
This is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger, like adding 'definitely' in English

steps.m: indeed he (referring to the Quran or Prophet ﷺ)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who the word is talking about. The small و (ۥ) is just to help with pronunciation.

steps.m: to cover, hide, or make unseen
possessed (literally: one whose mind is covered/hidden)
steps.n: The pattern مَفْعُول shows someone/something affected by the action. Here it means someone whose mind is covered/hidden by spirits, which is how Arabic describes possession.
The tanween (ٌ) shows this is talking about 'a' possessed person, not 'the' possessed person
The لَ adds emphasis like saying 'surely' or 'indeed'. This word is what they are saying in يَقُولُونَ (they say).

steps.m: what (used to make this statement negative)
steps.n: This is a special word in Arabic that can be used in many ways - to ask questions, to make statements negative, or to connect ideas
The و at the start means 'and' - it connects this phrase to what came before it
This is one of the most basic pronouns in Arabic - it's used to say 'he' or 'it' when talking about masculine things
Added the damma (ُ) on ه and fatha (َ) on و to show proper pronunciation. In this ayah, هُوَ refers back to the Quran, showing that the Quran is nothing but a reminder for all the worlds.
This is a particle that creates exceptions or restrictions. Here it connects 'and it is not' (وَمَا هُوَ) with 'a reminder' (ذِكْرٌ) to say 'it is nothing except/only a reminder'
Added the vowels ِ (kasra) under first letter and ْ (sukun) on middle letter to change from action to noun

steps.m: a reminder/remembrance (that comes before لِّلْعٰلَمِينَ - for all the worlds)
steps.n: Added ٌ (double damma) at the end to show this is indefinite - like saying 'a reminder' instead of 'the reminder'. This word is connected to لِّلْعٰلَمِينَ which tells us who the reminder is for.

steps.m: to know deeply or mark something

steps.n: Added alif to create a noun meaning 'that which is known' - referring to all of creation as something that can be known or marked by its signs
Added ين to show we're talking about multiple worlds or realms
Added لِ (li) to show this is 'for' or 'to' the worlds
Adds emphasis to show this is definitely/surely for all worlds
